sectionDescription: Rapid-fire numeric recall and abnormal scenario questions for IPC and airline interview preparation.

categories.title: Quick Fire Drill
categories.description: High-pressure numeric recall and decision-making scenarios.

modules.title: Fuel & Alternate Triggers
modules.summary: Key fuel definitions and take-off alternate trigger numbers.

content: 1 hour OEI cruise — typical maximum distance to take-off alternate (twin turbine)
30 or 45 minutes — final reserve fuel holding time (varies by regulation)
1,500 ft — holding altitude above aerodrome for final reserve calculation

Q: When is a take-off alternate required under IFR? A: When weather at departure aerodrome is below landing minima, or if return to departure would not be possible due to weather, performance or runway constraints.
Q: Define trip fuel. A: Fuel required from take-off to landing at the destination aerodrome.
Q: Define contingency fuel. A: Fuel carried to account for unforeseen factors such as wind errors, ATC delays or minor route changes.
Q: Define final reserve fuel. A: Fuel required to fly for a specified period (e.g. 30 or 45 minutes) at holding speed at 1,500 ft above aerodrome elevation.
Q: What is isolated aerodrome fuel logic? A: Additional fuel carried when no suitable alternate is available within range, ensuring safe diversion or extended holding capability.
Q: If RVR is reported for multiple touchdown zones, which value governs? A: The controlling (lowest required) RVR value applicable to the runway segment used.

Q: What's the typical maximum distance to a take-off alternate in a twin-engine turbine under Part 121? A: Generally within one hour at one-engine inoperative cruise speed (unless specific approvals exist).

modules.title: Approach Minima & Recency
modules.summary: Precision vs non-precision minima and recency requirements.

content: Q: What defines a precision approach? A: An approach providing both lateral and vertical guidance meeting ICAO precision standards (e.g., ILS).
Q: What is the key operational difference between MDA and DA? A: At DA a decision must be made immediately; at MDA descent must not continue below the altitude without required visual reference.
Q: What restores instrument rating recency if lapsed? A: Completion of required instrument approaches and time under supervision or successful completion of an IPC.

Q: Explain the difference between currency and proficiency. A: Currency is meeting regulatory recency requirements; proficiency is actual competence and skill level. Being current does not automatically mean proficient.
Confusing 'current' with 'competent' — meeting recency requirements doesn't guarantee skill level.
Treating DA as a 'look-and-see' altitude — it's a decision point, not a level-off altitude.

Q: You lose attitude indication in IMC. What is your immediate priority? A: Maintain aircraft control using remaining reliable instruments, cross-check with standby instruments, reduce workload, and stabilise flight before troubleshooting.

Q: Would you continue an approach partial panel to minima? A: Only if aircraft control is fully stabilised, workload manageable and risk acceptable. Otherwise climb, declare as required and reposition or divert.
Q: RAIM warning inside IAF on RNAV approach. What do you do? A: Discontinue the approach unless alternate approved navigation exists. Advise ATC if integrity not restored within required time.
Q: Why is GNSS integrity more important than raw accuracy? A: Because without integrity monitoring, the system cannot guarantee containment within obstacle protection surfaces.
Attempting to troubleshoot before stabilising the aircraft — aviate first.
Continuing an approach without RAIM integrity — integrity is non-negotiable.
Assuming 'accuracy' alone is sufficient — integrity ensures the position is within safe limits.

modules.title: Performance & Engine Failure
modules.summary: ATPL-level performance concepts and engine failure decision-making.

content: 3.3% — standard diverse departure climb gradient
V1 — decision speed: go or stop decision must be made by this point

Q: Engine failure immediately after V1 in low visibility. What is the first priority? A: Maintain directional control, continue the take-off, achieve safe climb performance, comply with published engine-out procedure and stabilise before any non-essential actions.

Q: Would you immediately plan to return after V1 engine failure? A: Only after stabilising, completing memory items, assessing performance, weather and runway suitability. A rushed return in marginal visibility can compound risk.

Q: How would you explain 'balanced field length' in plain language? A: It's where the distance required to continue after engine failure at V1 equals the distance to reject and stop. The runway gives symmetrical options.

Q: If a departure requires higher-than-standard climb gradient, what are your three levers? A: Reduce weight, wait for better conditions (lower temp/headwind), or choose a different departure with lower gradient requirement.

Q: How does a wet/contaminated runway change your take-off performance thinking? A: It increases accelerate-stop distance and may change accelerate-go assumptions. Contaminated conditions usually call for lower weight, more conservative decision points, or delay.

Q: How would you explain second segment climb in practical terms? A: It's engine-out climb performance after gear up and flap retraction, up to a specified height. It must clear obstacles while meeting the required climb gradient on one engine.
Treating any long runway as 'performance fine' without checking accelerate-stop/go distances.
Rushing to return after V1 engine failure — stabilise first.
Assuming visual departure workaround solves gradient problems if obstacles still exist.
